meta.title: Nephrology Literature Update
meta.query: Clinical nephrology — RCTs, systematic reviews & meta-analyses (humans, English)
meta.source: Generated by Google NotebookLM, grounded in PubMed (NLM) abstracts. Each entry links to its PubMed record and DOI.
meta.note: Summaries and PICO are NotebookLM analyses of the source abstracts; criteria marked “not specified” were not stated.

articles.authors: Davis JS, Robinson OJ, Walls G, Cheng MP, Yahav D, et al.
articles.title: Benzylpenicillin versus flucloxacillin or cloxacillin for the treatment of penicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus bacteraemia (SNAP): an international, multicentre, open-label, non-inferiority randomised controlled trial.
articles.summary: Benzylpenicillin was compared to anti-staphylococcal penicillins (flucloxacillin or cloxacillin) for PSSA bacteraemia, showing non-inferiority for 90-day mortality (14% vs 22%; adjusted OR 0.67, 95% CrI 0.35-1.28). Significantly, benzylpenicillin reduced the risk of acute kidney injury compared to the flucloxacillin/cloxacillin group (11% vs 22%; adjusted OR 0.50, 95% CrI 0.26-0.94). The trial was stopped early due to the increased AKI observed in the flucloxacillin/cloxacillin arm.

pico.population: Adults with penicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus (PSSA) bacteraemia.
pico.intervention: Intravenous benzylpenicillin (standard dosing 1.8 g q4h or 2.4 g q6h).
pico.comparison: Intravenous flucloxacillin or cloxacillin (2.0 g q6h or q4h).
pico.outcome: All-cause mortality at 90 days and incidence of acute kidney injury (AKI).

appraisal.bottom_line: Benzylpenicillin reduces the risk of AKI by half (OR 0.50) compared to flucloxacillin or cloxacillin while maintaining non-inferior survival in PSSA bacteraemia.
appraisal.relevance: Practicing nephrologists should advocate for switching from anti-staphylococcal penicillins to benzylpenicillin in confirmed PSSA cases to reduce nephrotoxicity.
appraisal.caveat: The trial was terminated early for safety, meaning it did not reach its full prespecified power for mortality non-inferiority.

articles.authors: Chuang MH, Ho CW, Wang HY, Pan HC, Wu VC, et al.
articles.title: Cardiovascular and renal outcomes of combined SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists versus monotherapy in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus: a network meta-analysis.
articles.summary: Combined SGLT2i and GLP-1RA therapy did not significantly reduce the risk of major adverse kidney events compared to SGLT2i alone (RR 1.05, 95% CI 0.74 to 1.49) or GLP-1RA alone (RR 0.86, 95% CI 0.60 to 1.21). However, combined therapy was associated with a lower risk of heart failure-related hospital admissions than SGLT2i monotherapy (RR 0.72, 95% CI 0.52 to 0.99) or GLP-1RA monotherapy (RR 0.62, 95% CI 0.45 to 0.86).

pico.intervention: Combined SGLT2 inhibitor and GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy.
pico.outcome: Major adverse kidney events (eGFR decline, kidney failure, death) and cardiovascular events.

inclusion: Randomized controlled trials of SGLT2i or GLP-1RA

appraisal.bottom_line: Combining SGLT2i and GLP-1RA does not further reduce major renal or cardiovascular events compared to monotherapy but does significantly lower heart failure hospitalizations.
appraisal.relevance: In patients with diabetes and high heart failure risk, combination therapy is beneficial, though the renal benefit appears similar to using either agent alone.
appraisal.caveat: Results for primary outcomes were based on very low certainty evidence and indirect comparisons.

articles.authors: Li Y, Zheng R, Liu X, Zheng J, Lu C, et al.
articles.title: Fractional Flow Reserve-Guided Renal Artery Stenting in Renovascular Hypertension: 1-Year Results of the FAIR Study.
articles.summary: FFR-guided revascularization for atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis reduced the stenting rate by 54% (46% vs 100%, P < 0.01) without sacrificing blood pressure control or renal safety at 1 year. Significant 12-month eGFR improvement was only observed in the subgroup with FFR <0.80 that underwent stenting.

pico.population: Adults with atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis (ARAS).
pico.intervention: FFR-guided renal artery stenting (stent only if FFR <0.80).
pico.comparison: Angiography-guided renal artery stenting (stent for all).
pico.outcome: Ambulatory daytime mean systolic blood pressure (DMSBP) and renal outcomes (eGFR).

appraisal.bottom_line: Using FFR <0.80 to guide renal stenting safely reduces stent use by more than half while identifying the subset most likely to experience eGFR improvement.
appraisal.relevance: FFR should be considered the standard for determining the functional significance of renal artery stenosis before proceeding with revascularization.
appraisal.caveat: This was a pilot trial with a relatively small sample size (n=107).

articles.authors: Schenker N, Borof K, Goette A, Schmidt-Lauber C, Breithardt G, et al.
articles.title: Effectiveness and Safety of Early Rhythm Control in Patients With Atrial Fibrillation and Chronic Kidney Disease.
articles.summary: In patients with recently diagnosed AF and stroke risk factors, early rhythm control (ERC) reduced the primary composite cardiovascular outcome in those with CKD (HR 0.67; 5.8% vs 8.5% events/100 patient-years) and without CKD (HR 0.84; 3.4% vs 4.1% events/100 patient-years). CKD patients experienced more AF recurrences with usual care, and ERC was found to be safe across the GFR spectrum.

pico.population: Patients with recently diagnosed AF and stroke risk factors, stratified by CKD status (GFR <60).
pico.outcome: Composite of CV death, stroke, heart failure hospitalization, or acute coronary syndrome.

appraisal.bottom_line: Early rhythm control for AF is both safe and effective in reducing major cardiovascular events in patients with CKD (HR 0.67).
appraisal.relevance: CKD should not be a barrier to initiating early rhythm control in patients with recently diagnosed atrial fibrillation.
appraisal.caveat: This is a secondary analysis, and CKD patients were generally older with more comorbidities than the non-CKD group.

articles.authors: Volis I, Gruber A, Abbo AR, Aronson D, Girerd N, et al.
articles.title: Kidney function and acid-base status as gatekeepers of natriuretic and diuretic responsiveness in chronic HF: insights from the DEA-HF clinical trial.
articles.summary: In heart failure patients with eGFR >30, adding metolazone to furosemide significantly increased 6-hour natriuresis compared to furosemide alone (5525 mg vs 4303 mg, P=0.014), whereas acetazolamide did not. Conversely, in patients with eGFR ≤30, neither adjunct diuretic provided additional benefit over furosemide monotherapy.

pico.population: Patients with congestion-refractory chronic heart failure.
pico.intervention: Metolazone (5mg) + IV furosemide (250mg) OR Acetazolamide (500mg) + IV furosemide (250mg).

appraisal.bottom_line: Metolazone effectively boosts natriuresis in congestion-refractory HF only when eGFR >30 mL/min/1.73m2, whereas neither metolazone nor acetazolamide helps if eGFR is ≤30.
appraisal.relevance: Adjunct diuretic choices in heart failure should be stratified by renal function; metolazone is less useful in advanced CKD.
appraisal.caveat: The study was a small post-hoc analysis (n=42) and results for clinical decongestion did not reach significance.

articles.authors: Tsioufis K, Kyriakoulis KG, Vakka A, Stamataki N, Tatakis F, et al.
articles.title: Single Interventions (Pharmacological or Renal Denervation) Are Not Sufficient to Achieve Blood Pressure Control in Resistant Hypertension: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.summary: Meta-analysis of 58 studies (n=4579) showed that adding a single intervention (pharmacotherapy or renal denervation) to baseline therapy for resistant hypertension reduced 24-hour SBP by -11.3 mm Hg (95% CI, -12.3 to -10.2). However, the pooled rate of achieving blood pressure control was only 35% (95% CI, 28-42%).

pico.intervention: Addition of a single pharmacological or device-based (RDN) intervention.
pico.outcome: 24-hour ambulatory systolic blood pressure reduction and hypertension control rate.

inclusion: Addition of single pharmacological or device-based intervention

appraisal.bottom_line: A single add-on intervention for resistant hypertension provides substantial BP reduction but only achieves targets in one-third of patients.
appraisal.relevance: Nephrologists should favor combination strategies or optimized multi-drug regimens over single-intervention approaches for resistant hypertension.
appraisal.caveat: Results were primarily from a single-arm framework meta-analysis with significant heterogeneity in study quality.

articles.authors: Wang X, Liu Y, Wang Z, Wang H, Li C, et al.
articles.title: Effects of early continuous renal replacement therapy in critically ill patients requiring ECMO treatment: results of a randomised controlled trial.
articles.summary: This multicentre trial randomized ECMO patients to early CRRT (within 24 hours) or conventional initiation. No significant difference was found in 30-day mortality (43.5% in early group vs 46.7% in usual care; HR 0.93, 95% CI 0.50 to 1.72) or serious adverse events.

pico.population: Critically ill patients undergoing extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO).
pico.intervention: Early initiation of CRRT (within 24 hours post-ECMO).

appraisal.bottom_line: Early initiation of CRRT in ECMO patients does not reduce 30-day mortality compared to a wait-and-see approach based on clinical indications.
appraisal.relevance: Nephrologists should continue to use conventional triggers for starting CRRT in the setting of ECMO rather than proactive early initiation.
appraisal.caveat: The trial was terminated early and was substantially underpowered (n=91) to detect meaningful differences.

articles.authors: Liu M, Wu Y, Ye F, Liu W, Deng X, et al.
articles.title: Risk prediction models for malnutrition in dialysis patients in China: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.summary: In a meta-analysis of 12 studies, the pooled prevalence of malnutrition among dialysis patients was 41%. Eight significant predictors were identified, including age, Kt/V, serum calcium, and comorbid diabetes, with internal validation models showing good discriminatory performance (AUC 0.83).

pico.outcome: Prevalence of malnutrition and model discriminatory performance (AUC).

inclusion: Reporting risk prediction models for malnutrition

appraisal.bottom_line: Malnutrition affects 41% of dialysis patients, and models using predictors like age and Kt/V can effectively identify high-risk individuals (AUC 0.83).
appraisal.relevance: Predictive factors such as Kt/V and diabetes should be routinely monitored to screen for and intervene in malnutrition in dialysis units.
appraisal.caveat: All included models were rated at high risk of bias due to methodological and reporting limitations.
